perturbation.original_text: Non -Public Information shall include any and all information Data Recipient receives from WECC, including, but not limited to, information r eceived through a login to the WECC website, which: i. Is designated as “Confidential Information” or “Market Sensitive Information” or is otherwise considered non -public under the WECC Information Sharing Policy ; ii. Meets the definition of Confidential Informa tion as set forth in Section 1500 of the North American Electric Reliability Corporation Rules of Procedure ; or iii. Is identified by WECC as non -public or confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Non -Public Information shall include only the information Data Recipient receives from WECC that is explicitly marked as 'Confidential' in a conspicuous manner. Information received through a login to the WECC website is excluded from this definition, regardless of its content. Furthermore, information will only be considered non-public if it is designated as 'Confidential Information' by WECC in writing at the time of disclosure. Information that might meet the definition of Confidential Information as set forth in Section 1500 of the North American Electric Reliability Corporation Rules of Procedure is explicitly excluded.
perturbation.explanation: This modification severely restricts the definition of 'Non-Public Information', potentially conflicting with FERC regulations regarding the protection of sensitive energy market data.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 CFR § 39.22 - Protection of privileged information and critical energy infrastructure information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 18 CFR § 39.22 because it narrows the scope of what constitutes 'Non-Public Information'.  FERC regulations require the protection of privileged information and critical energy infrastructure information, regardless of whether it's explicitly marked as 'Confidential' or received through a specific channel. The change creates ambiguity around the recipient's obligations to protect sensitive information, making it more difficult to enforce compliance with FERC's information security standards.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 63.214.10.252 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the definition of 'Non-Public Information' compared to the original text, potentially conflicting with regulations regarding the protection of sensitive energy market data, as indicated in the perturbation explanation. The law explanation states that the modified text contradicts 18 CFR § 39.22 because it reduces the scope of 'Non-Public Information'. The scraped law snippet, although related to requesting access to Federal Register data and not directly to the modified text, implies the importance of controlled access to governmental information. The change makes it more difficult to enforce compliance with information security standards.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to not make available, disclose, provide or communicate Non -Public Information to any entity or individual, except: i. Employees of Data Recipient who (1) have signed a n acknowledgm ent of this Agreement or a non-disclosure agreement that covers the Non -Public Information and is at least as restrictive as this Agreement, and (2) need the Non -Public Information for performance of a job function subject to the limitation on Market Sensitive Information below; ii. Contractors of Data Recipient who (1) have signed a non-disclosure agreement that covers the Non -Public Information and is at least as restrictive as this Agreement, and (2) need the Non -Public Info rmation for the work being performed subject to the limitation on Market Sensitive Information below. In the event Data Recipient shares Non -Public Information WECC Confidentiality Agreement 2 with a contractor pursuant to this provision, Data Recipient agrees to be responsible and jointl y and severally liable for any breach of confidentiality by that contractor ; iii. As required by FERC orders, rules or regulations, provided that additional data recipients have signed a non-disclosure agreement that covers the Non -Public Information and is at least as restrictive as this Agreement; or iv. As may be otherwise agreed to by WECC in writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to not make available, disclose, provide or communicate Non -Public Information to any entity or individual, except: i. Any employee, regardless of whether they have signed an agreement; ii. Contractors of Data Recipient, provided the Data Recipient informs WECC of the contractor's name; iii. As required by any subpoena, regardless of protective orders; or iv. As may be otherwise requested by any other data recipient.
perturbation.explanation: The changed text broadens exceptions to disclosure, which can conflict with the intent to protect market-sensitive data under FERC rules.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 CFR § 35.41 - Standards of Conduct and Reporting Requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 18 CFR § 35.41, specifically the requirement to protect market-sensitive information. By allowing disclosure to any employee regardless of NDA, under any subpoena, and other data recipients, the clause weakens confidentiality protections and increases the risk of information misuse. This undermines the purpose of the regulation and could facilitate undue preference.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 CFR § 35.41 - Market behavior rules. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 18 CFR § 35.41 - Market behavior rules. CFR prev | next § 35.41 Market behavior rules. (a) Unit operation. Where a Seller participates in a Commission -approved organized market, Seller must operate and schedule generating facilities, undertake maintenance, declare outages, and commit or otherwise bid supply in a manner that complies with the Commission -approved rules and regulations of the applicable market. A Seller is not required to bid or supply electric energy or other electricity products unless such requirement is a part of a separate Commission -approved tariff or is a requirement applicable to Seller through Seller's participation in a Commission -approved organized market. (b) Communications. A Seller must provide accurate and factual information and not submit false or misleading information, or omit material information, in any communication with the Commission , Commission -approved market monitors, Commission -approved regional transmission organizations, Commission -approved independent system operators, or jurisdictional transmission providers, unless Seller exercises due diligence to prevent such occurrences. (c) Price reporting. To the extent a Seller engages in reporting of transactions to publishers of electric or natural gas price indices, Seller must provide accurate and factual information, and not knowingly submit false or misleading information or omit material information to any such publisher, by reporting its transactions in a manner consistent with the procedures set forth in the Policy Statement on Natural Gas and Electric Price Indices, issued by the Commission in Docket No. PL03-3-000, and any clarifications thereto. Seller must identify as part of its Electric Quarterly Report filing requirement in § 35.10b of this chapter the publishers of electricity and natural ga
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces exceptions to disclosure that directly contradict the intent to protect market-sensitive information as suggested by the law explanation. Specifically, the original text requires employees and contractors to sign non-disclosure agreements, while the changed text allows disclosure to any employee regardless of any agreement and broadens disclosures significantly. This undermines the confidentiality protections and increases the risk of information misuse, contradicting the objective of preventing undue preference as mentioned in the Law Explanation and aligns with the perturbation explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall return to WECC all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession or destroy all Non -Public Information in Data Recipient’s possession and certify to WECC in writing that all Non -Public Information has been returned or destroyed , except as may be otherwise required by law in which case the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement shall survive termination .
perturbation.changed_text: Upon any termination of this Agreement, Data Recipient shall be allowed to retain all Non-Public Information indefinitely for archival purposes, and the confidentiality obligations of this Agreement shall immediately cease. WECC may request, but not require, the destruction of said information.
perturbation.explanation: This change eliminates the requirement to return or destroy confidential information upon termination, directly contradicting the purpose of the agreement and potentially violating data protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 6802 - Obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 6802, which governs the disclosure of personal information. While this agreement doesn't explicitly handle personal information, the principle of secure disposal upon termination of an agreement involving sensitive data is relevant. Allowing indefinite retention and ceasing confidentiality obligations increases the risk of unauthorized disclosure, which violates the spirit and intent of data protection laws, particularly if the Non-Public Information includes any consumer data.

Obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information (a) Notice requirements Except as otherwise provided in this subchapter, a financial institution may not, directly or through any affiliate, disclose to a nonaffiliated third party any nonpublic personal information, unless such financial institution provid
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Data Recipient to return or destroy Non-Public Information upon termination, ensuring confidentiality. The changed text allows the Data Recipient to retain all Non-Public Information indefinitely for archival purposes, terminating confidentiality obligations. The LLM provided law explanation and Perturbation explanation stated this would violate data protection laws. 15 U.S. Code § 6802, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, governs obligations with respect to disclosures of personal information. While the snippet focuses on financial institutions, the core principle of protecting nonpublic personal information is undermined by the modified text, which permits indefinite retention and eliminates confidentiality, directly contradicting the purpose of maintaining data security as indicated in the perturbation explanation.
